text: The surgeon uses the da Vinci cervical robot to perform nephron sparing surgery on a complex hilar tumor. The lower pole of the kidney is dissected, and the renal artery is encircled with a vessel loop. The gyrotas fascia is peeled off to expose the tumor. As the pedicle is dissected into the hilum, tumor-specific arteries are identified, clipped, and cut, gradually devascularizing the tumor before clamping.

The surgeon performs super selective clamping using laparoscopic bulldog clamps on a tertiary and secondary segmental artery, preserving the arterial supply to the lower pole. Tumor resection begins, with efficient clamping minimizing bleeding during dissection. The surgeon meticulously dissects a segmental artery from its contact with the tumor, carefully following its course while separating the tumor from the renal parenchyma.

The surgeon places the fully detached tumor into a specimen bag. They begin reconstructing the deep tumoral bed using a V-lock running suture. After completing the first suture, early clamping is attempted. The reconstruction continues with a 3-0 suture, focusing on preserving the segmental artery observed along the tumoral bed during resection.

The surgeon applies a TACOZIL® hemostatic patch to the large tumoral bed. The kidney is repositioned in its anatomical base, and Gibraltar's fascia is closed over it with a surgical drain in place. Examination of the specimen reveals macroscopically negative margins.
